items.front: In what order are IFR clearance items issued?
items.back: Issue aircraft identification, clearance limit, SID or vectors, route, altitude data, Mach if applicable, holding, special information, then frequency and beacon code.

items.front: How is a clearance or advisory prefixed when it is relayed through a non-ATC facility?
items.back: Prefix it with phrases such as "ATC clears," "ATC advises," or "ATC requests."

items.front: What should be included if clearance delivery instructions are needed?
items.back: Issue specific clearance delivery instructions as appropriate.

items.front: How must a clearance be relayed?
What must be restated when a previously issued route or altitude clearance is amended?
items.back: Restate all applicable altitude restrictions, and if a speed restriction cannot be met with an earlier altitude restriction, issue an amended clearance instead of vague wording.

items.front: May ATC clear an aircraft through intermediate stops?
items.back: Yes. You may clear an aircraft through intermediate stops.

items.front: What phrase is used when an aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?
items.back: Use the phrase "via approved altitude reservation flight plan."

items.front: How are flights handled when they switch between IFR and VFR?
items.back: IFR-then-VFR flights are cleared to the fix where IFR ends. VFR-then-IFR flights depart VFR and receive IFR near the change point, with beacon and terrain-clearance procedures applied as required.

items.front: What is the basic processing rule for an airfile aircraft?
items.back: Ensure it is in your jurisdiction unless coordinated, obtain the information needed for IFR service, and issue a destination, short-range, or FSS-contact clearance as appropriate.

items.front: What must be provided when an arriving IFR aircraft needs to cancel at an airport without a tower or FSS?
items.back: Before switching the pilot to CTAF if needed, tell the pilot how to report IFR cancellation, and acknowledge the cancellation when received.
